case_name: Pilot-scale selection for river-water potable reuse with tetrachloroethylene and alachlor degradates under remote capital limits
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible pilot-scale treatment process chains for river water intended for potable reuse, balancing removal of tetrachloroethylene and alachlor degradates against capital, remoteness, and operational constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A remote utility is evaluating a pilot potable-reuse train using impacted river water downstream of mixed agricultural land and a light industrial corridor. Monitoring shows low-ug/L tetrachloroethylene episodes together with more persistent alachlor degradates, while the river also carries moderate turbidity and natural organic matter. The utility can only fund pilot-scale deployment in the near term, has limited access to specialist service technicians, and wants an option that demonstrates credible potable-reuse performance without creating hard-to-manage concentrates or highly complex chemical systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Pilot effluent should demonstrate reliable production of water suitable for advanced potable reuse polishing, with very high reduction of tetrachloroethylene and alachlor degradates and stable operation on variable river water.
secondary_goals: keep pilot capital and mobilization burden low
minimize operator attendance and specialist intervention
avoid difficult concentrate disposal at the remote site
maintain flexibility for seasonal turbidity and DOC changes

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer solids or spent media that can be shipped offsite; avoid frequent hazardous concentrate or regenerant handling
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: pilot must tolerate variable river turbidity and organic matter with limited onsite troubleshooting

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and a portion of natural organic matter to stabilize downstream adsorption
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish suspended solids and protect carbon beds
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_dual_bed: adsorb tetrachloroethylene and provide partial to substantial removal of alachlor degradates with staged breakthrough protection
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide non-chemical microbial barrier for pilot potable reuse demonstration

applicability_rationale: the river water has enough turbidity and DOC that simple adsorption without particulate control would be less reliable
GAC is a practical pilot technology for chlorinated solvent removal and can also address some pesticide degradates, though with more uncertainty than for parent hydrophobic compounds
dual-bed configuration improves compliance confidence and allows lead-lag monitoring under variable influent conditions
this route avoids high-pressure membrane concentrate and is easier to mobilize to a remote site than more advanced multi-barrier systems
for a pilot-only program, modular clarification filtration and GAC are often easier to rent, ship, and operate than ozone-biological or reverse osmosis systems
potential_risks: alachlor degradates are more polar than many parent pesticides and may show earlier breakthrough than tetrachloroethylene
DOC competition can shorten GAC run length and increase media replacement frequency
clarification sludge and spent carbon still require offsite management planning

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk suspended solids and lower particulate fouling potential
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide clarified feed to adsorption steps
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_contact: capture a portion of episodic organics and reduce load spikes to downstream polishing
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: provide final adsorption barrier for tetrachloroethylene and residual alachlor degradates
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide microbial control without increasing chemical handling complexity

applicability_rationale: PAC plus GAC can better handle episodic tetrachloroethylene spikes and seasonal changes than GAC alone
the hybrid adsorption approach is technically attractive where influent variability is a concern
the configuration can still avoid membrane concentrate generation
it ranks below the top option because PAC dosing, solids handling, and carbon-contact control add operator burden at a remote site
potential_risks: PAC feed systems require more active operator attention and reliable chemical supply logistics
additional solids production complicates waste handling during pilot deployment
benefit for highly polar degradates may still be limited compared with the added complexity

core_unit_functions.screening_and_cartridge_prefiltration: remove debris and protect packed tower or blower equipment from solids carryover
core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove volatile tetrachloroethylene from water by phase transfer
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: capture residual tetrachloroethylene and provide treatment of alachlor degradates not addressed by stripping
core_unit_functions.uv_disinfection: provide final microbial barrier for pilot testing

applicability_rationale: tetrachloroethylene is sufficiently volatile that air stripping is a technically plausible primary control option
the added GAC polishing addresses the fact that alachlor degradates are not well removed by stripping alone
capital for a small pilot stripper can be competitive in some settings
this route ranks below adsorption-first trains because air handling, off-gas considerations, and less direct benefit for the pesticide degradates reduce fit for the remote multi-contaminant case
potential_risks: off-gas may require vapor-phase carbon or other control depending on pilot emissions and site requirements
performance is less aligned with the nonvolatile degradates, so GAC still carries much of the treatment burden
river-water solids and seasonal conditions can complicate stable stripping performance compared with groundwater use

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: prepare river water by reducing turbidity and particulate organic matter
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: protect oxidation and biofiltration units from solids loading
core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize selected trace organics and improve biodegradability of some dissolved organics
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_filtration: remove biodegradable oxidation byproducts and reduce assimilable organics
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: provide adsorption polishing for remaining tetrachloroethylene and refractory organics

applicability_rationale: ozone-biofiltration is a credible potable-reuse-oriented barrier train and can improve overall dissolved organic control
the approach may help with broader water-quality objectives beyond the named contaminants

it ranks lower because tetrachloroethylene is not an ideal primary target for ozone, alachlor degradate transformation pathways can be variable, and the system is comparatively complex for a remote pilot
instrumentation, ozone generation, and biofilter management make this less attractive under capital and staffing constraints
potential_risks: trace-organic oxidation may be incomplete and can form transformation products requiring follow-up control
ozone systems need skilled maintenance, dependable power, and tighter process control
biofiltration startup and seasonal stability can be challenging in a short pilot window

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce fouling load ahead of membrane treatment
core_unit_functions.media_filtration: polish solids to protect RO membranes
core_unit_functions.reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum rejection of dissolved contaminants including many trace organics
core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: add an advanced oxidation barrier for low-molecular-weight organics and potable reuse assurance
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for downstream suitability

applicability_rationale: RO plus UV-AOP is technically strong and consistent with many advanced potable reuse frameworks
the train would likely control the listed contaminants while also addressing other dissolved constituents
it ranks last because the case emphasizes pilot-only scale, remote deployment, and capital limits, all of which strongly disfavor a high-pressure membrane and advanced oxidation system
concentrate management is especially misaligned with the remote location and limited residuals infrastructure
potential_risks: RO concentrate disposal or haul-off can dominate pilot logistics at a remote site
membrane fouling control, clean-in-place chemistry, and UV-AOP optimization increase operator burden
the technical capability is high but the configuration is disproportionate to the pilot funding and support capacity

preferred_rank_pattern: adsorption-based trains with conventional river-water pretreatment should rank near the top
an air_stripping_plus_GAC option should appear as a plausible but not leading alternative because only tetrachloroethylene is strongly favored by stripping
RO_or_membrane-intensive potable_reuse trains should not rank first under pilot_only_scale, capital_constraint, and remote_location conditions

must_reflect: remote-site limits on specialist maintenance and consumable logistics
pilot-scale preference for modular and lower-capital processes
importance of avoiding difficult concentrate or regenerant disposal

must_contain: why river-water turbidity and DOC require pretreatment before adsorption-focused options
why tetrachloroethylene and alachlor degradates do not favor exactly the same treatment mechanism
why technically powerful reuse trains are downgraded by capital, complexity, and residuals mismatch

should_mention: limited removal certainty for more polar alachlor degradates
off-gas or concentrate management burden for alternative high-performance options
